findings.title: Ransomware Actors Exploit Unpatched SimpleHelp Remote Monitoring and Management to Compromise Utility Billing Software Provider
findings.summary: Summary The Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) is releasing this advisory in response to ransomware actors leveraging unpatched instances of a vulnerability in SimpleHelp Remote Monitoring and Management (RMM) to compromise customers of a utility billing software provider. This incident reflects a broader pattern of ransomware actors targeting organizations through unpatched versions of SimpleHelp RMM since January 2025. SimpleHelp versions 5.5.7 and earlier contain several vulnerabilities, including CVE-2024-57727 —a path traversal vulnerability. 1 Ransomware actors likely leveraged CVE-2024-57727 to access downstream customers’ unpatched SimpleHelp RMM for disruption of services in double extortion compromises. 1 CISA added CVE-2024-57727 to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) Catalog on Feb. 13, 2025. CISA urges software vendors, downstream customers, and end users to immediately implement the Mitigations listed in this advisory based on confirmed compromise or risk of compromise. Download the PDF version of this report: AA25-163A Ransomware Actors Exploit Unpatched SimpleHelp Remote Monitoring and Management to Compromise Utility Billing Software Provider (PDF, 420.49 KB ) Mitigations CISA recommends organizations implement the mitigations below to respond to emerging ransomware activity exploiting SimpleHelp software. These mitigations align with the Cross-Sector Cybersecurity Performance Goals (CPGs) developed by CISA and the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST). The CPGs provide a minimum set of practices and protections that CISA and NIST recommend all organizations implement. CISA and NIST based the CPGs on existing cybersecurity frameworks and guidance to protect against the most common and impactful threats, tactics, techniques, and procedures. Visit CISA’s CPGs webpage for more information on the CPGs, including additional recommended baseline protections. These mitigations apply to all critical infrastructure

enrichment.technical_details: CWE-22: Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory ('Path Traversal'). SimpleHelp Remote Support/RMM versions 5.5.7 and earlier contain multiple path-traversal flaws that allow unauthenticated remote attackers to download arbitrary files from the SimpleHelp host via specially crafted HTTP requests. Readable files include the server configuration file containing secrets and hashed user (admin) passwords. Attackers retrieve the admin password hash, crack it offline, then chain it with other flaws (e.g., CVE-2024-57726 upload) to obtain remote code execution as a technician/SYSTEM, which they use to deploy ransomware to the RMM host and pivot to downstream MSP customers.
enrichment.affected_products: SimpleHelp Remote Monitoring and Management (RMM) / Remote Support software, version 5.5.7 and all earlier releases (including 5.4.x and 5.3.x branches).
enrichment.exploit_available: Yes - a public proof-of-concept is available. Repository: https://github.com/imjdl/CVE-2024-57727 (Python-based vulnerability scanner/checker). A weaponized exploit is also being actively used in the wild by DragonForce ransomware actors.
enrichment.patch_available: Yes - SimpleHelp released patches in January 2025, approximately two days after responsible disclosure and one week before public disclosure. Fixed versions: SimpleHelp 5.5.8, 5.4.10, and 5.3.9 (or any later release). Vendor advisory: https://simple-help.com/blogs/security-vulnerabilities-in-simplehelp-5-5-7-and-earlier-what-you-need-to-know and Knowledge Base: https://guides.simple-help.com/kb---security-vulnerabilities-01-2025
enrichment.active_exploitation: Yes - confirmed active exploitation in the wild. Sources: (1) CISA Cybersecurity Advisory AA25-163A (published June 12, 2025) - 'Ransomware Actors Exploit Unpatched SimpleHelp Remote Monitoring and Management to Compromise Utility Billing Software Provider' - documents DragonForce actors leveraging CVE-2024-57727 against unpatched SimpleHelp instances since January 2025 to breach a utility billing software provider and downstream customers in a double-extortion ransomware campaign. (2) Sophos (Anthony Bradshaw, May 27, 2025) - 'DragonForce actors target SimpleHelp vulnerabilities to attack MSP, customers' - reports DragonForce abusing SimpleHelp RMM to access multiple organizations, with Sophos EDR blocking encryption on at least one customer network. (3) Halcyon blog (June 24, 2025) - 'Unpatched SimpleHelp Vulnerabilities Continue to be Exploited by DragonForce'. (4) Horizon3.ai (January 13, 2025) technical disclosure of the underlying flaws. The vulnerability is listed in CISA's Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog.
enrichment.threat_actors: DragonForce (ransomware operator); Scattered Spider / UNC3944 (also reported by Sophos targeting SimpleHelp). CISA attributes ongoing exploitation since January 2025 to DragonForce in advisory AA25-163A.
enrichment.mitigation: 1) Immediately upgrade SimpleHelp to v5.5.8 (and matching v5.4.10 / v5.3.9 for older branches) - update BOTH the server AND every deployed Remote Access Service. 2) If you cannot patch immediately, isolate the SimpleHelp server instance from the internet or stop the server process. 3) Hunt for suspicious executables with three-letter filenames (e.g., aaa.exe, zzz.exe) created after January 2025 on any SimpleHelp server or downstream endpoint, and remove them. 4) Reset all SimpleHelp admin/user passwords and rotate any secrets stored in the server configuration. 5) Add SimpleHelp binaries to the endpoint security allowlist only after verifying integrity; do not pre-allow-list generic write locations. 6) Enforce network segmentation between the RMM server, the management network, and customer networks; do not expose SimpleHelp directly to the internet. 7) If a host was encrypted, disconnect it, wipe and reinstall the OS, and restore data from clean, offline backups. 8) Perform host and network vulnerability scans and threat hunting across downstream MSP customers.

findings.title: AI threats in the wild: The current state of prompt injections on the web
findings.summary: Posted by Thomas Brunner, Yu-Han Liu, Moni Pande At Google, our Threat Intelligence teams are dedicated to staying ahead of real-world adversarial activity, proactively monitoring emerging threats before they can impact users. Right now, Indirect Prompt Injection (IPI) is a top priority for the security community, anticipating it as a primary attack vector for adversaries to target and compromise AI agents. But while the danger of IPI is widely discussed, are threat actors actually exploiting this vector today – and if so, how? To answer these questions and to uncover real-world abuse, we initiated a broad sweep of the public web to monitor for known indirect prompt injection patterns. This is what we found. The threat of indirect prompt injection Unlike a direct injection where a user "jailbreaks" a chatbot, IPI occurs when an AI system processes content—like a website, email, or document—that contains malicious instructions. When the AI reads this poisoned content, it may silently follow the attacker's commands instead of the user's original intent. This is not a new area of concern for us and Google has been working tirelessly to combat these threats. Our efforts involve cross-functional collaboration between researchers at Google DeepMind (GDM) and defenders like the Google Threat Intelligence Group (GTIG). We have previously detailed our work in this area and researchers have further highlighted the evolving nature of these vulnerabilities. Despite this collective focus, a fundamental question remains: to what degree are real-world malicious actors currently operationalizing these attacks? Proactive monitoring at Google The landscape of IPI on the web There are many channels through which attackers might try to send prompt injections. However, one location is particularly easy to observe - the public web. Here, threat actors may simply seed prompt injections on websites in hope of corrupting AI systems that browse them. Public research confirms these attacks ar

enrichment.technical_details: Indirect Prompt Injection (IPI) attacks LLM-powered agents and assistants by causing untrusted external content (web pages, emails, documents, calendar invites, search-query history) to be processed as if it were direct instructions, overriding the system prompt's intended behavior. Adversaries defeat the data-vs-instruction boundary using techniques such as: invisible / off-screen text (1-pixel fonts, near-transparent colors, zero-width characters, aria-hidden / visually-hidden DOM nodes), instructions buried in HTML comments and page metadata, Base64-encoded instructions decoded at runtime, canvas-rendered text invisible to text analyzers, timed delays to evade scan pipelines, multilingual or JSON/code-style obfuscation, semantic social-engineering phrases ('Ignore previous instructions'), and JavaScript-triggered multi-search-query injection poisoning victim search history for subsequent personalization ingestion. Documented in-the-wild payloads include: tone-change pranks, SEO promotion, 'deterrence' payloads luring AI crawlers into infinite-stream pages, system-prompt leakage, sensitive-data exfiltration (memory, location, credentials, API keys), forced user redirects/phishing, instruction hijack, and destructive actions.
enrichment.affected_products: AI agents and LLM-powered assistants broadly; specifically Google Gemini (including Gemini Search Personalization), Google Bard, Microsoft Bing AI/Copilot, OpenAI ChatGPT, AI-powered indexers/crawlers, AI ad-review systems. No specific affected versions were published.
enrichment.exploit_available: Yes — multiple public PoCs exist. (1) Google's blog cites publicly known IPI research at https://greshake.github.io/ . (2) Tenable TRA-2025-23 (https://tenable.com/security/research/tra-2025-23) published a full PoC for Gemini Search Personalization search-history poisoning. (3) Open-source PoC at https://github.com/brennanbrown/atlas-prompt-injection-poc. (4) Forcepoint X-Labs published 10 in-the-wild payload samples (https://forcepoint.com/blog/x-labs/indirect-prompt-injection-payloads).
enrichment.patch_available: No CVE-style vendor patch was issued for the threat class discussed in the blog post itself (the vulnerability_id is a Blogger post ID, not a CVE). However, product-level fixes have been shipped in adjacent IPI issues: Gemini Search Personalization was rolled back and re-hardened after Tenable Research Advisory TRA-2025-23 (https://tenable.com/security/research/tra-2025-23); Gemini was patched for the calendar-invite authorization-bypass IPI disclosed by Miggo (January 2026); Microsoft 365 Copilot was patched for EchoLeak (CVE-2025-32711, CVSS 9.3) referenced as part of the same IPI threat family. The 2026-04-23 Google Security Blog post summarizes ongoing hardening rather than issuing a single CVE fix.
enrichment.active_exploitation: Yes — confirmed active exploitation in the wild. Google Security Blog (2026-04-23) reports Common Crawl telemetry showing a 32% increase in detected malicious-prompt-injection category between November 2025 and February 2026. Forcepoint X-Labs (2026-04-22) documented 10 distinct IPI payloads actively served to AI agents and crawlers in the wild, including financial-fraud, data-destruction, exfiltration, prompt-leakage, traffic-manipulation, and behavior-suppression intents. Palo Alto Networks Unit 42 (2026-03-03) confirmed covert web-based IPI observed against real AI-driven products. Independent confirmation also by Help Net Security (2026-04-24), Tenable (Gemini Search Personalization IPI, July 2025), and Miggo (Gemini calendar-invite IPI, January 2026).
enrichment.mitigation: Layered defenses are required because IPI has no universal patch: (1) Strict data-instruction boundary (e.g., 'spotlighting' to separate trusted from untrusted text). (2) Hierarchy of instructions, adversarial training, and pressure-testing red-teaming (Google runs continuous AI red-teaming and an AI Vulnerability Reward Program). (3) Real-time, web-scale detection (intent analysis, prompt-visibility assessment, behavioral correlation) using telemetry signals like Common Crawl. (4) Content Security Policy hardening, input validation/sanitization, and limiting agent capabilities. (5) Human-in-the-loop for destructive or financially significant actions. (6) Network-level / proxy-level blocking of IPI URLs (Forcepoint Real-Time Analytics; Palo Alto Networks Advanced URL Filtering, Advanced DNS Security, Prisma AIRS, Prisma Browser). (7) For users of Gemini-style personalization, clearing or disabling personalization history reduces the search-history-poisoning attack surface demonstrated in Tenable TRA-2025-23.

findings.title: Google Workspace’s continuous approach to mitigating indirect prompt injections
findings.summary: Posted by Adam Gavish, Google GenAI Security Team Indirect prompt injection (IPI) is an evolving threat vector targeting users of complex AI applications with multiple data sources, such as Workspace with Gemini. This technique enables the attacker to influence the behavior of an LLM by injecting malicious instructions into the data or tools used by the LLM as it completes the user’s query. This may even be possible without any input directly from the user. IPI is not the kind of technical problem you “solve” and move on. Sophisticated LLMs with increasing use of agentic automation combined with a wide range of content create an ultra-dynamic and evolving playground for adversarial attacks. That’s why Google takes a sophisticated and comprehensive approach to these attacks. We’re continuously improving LLM resistance to IPI attacks and launching AI application capabilities with ever-improving defenses. Staying ahead of the latest indirect prompt injection attacks is critical to our mission of securing Workspace with Gemini. In our previous blog “ Mitigating prompt injection attacks with a layered defense strategy ”, we reviewed the layered architecture of our IPI defenses. In this blog, we’ll share more detail on the continuous approach we take to improve these defenses and to solve for new attacks. New attack discovery By proactively discovering and cataloging new attack vectors through internal and external programs, we can identify vulnerabilities and deploy robust defenses ahead of adversarial activity. Human Red-Teaming Human Red-Teaming uses adversarial simulations to uncover security and safety vulnerabilities. Specialized teams execute attacks based on realistic user profiles to exploit weaknesses, coordinating with product teams to resolve identified issues. Automated Red-Teaming Automated Red-Teaming is done via dynamic, machine-learning-driven frameworks to stress-test environments. By algorithmically generating and iterating on attack payloads, we can mi

enrichment.technical_details: Indirect prompt injection (IPI) is a class of attack against LLM-based applications where an adversary plants malicious instructions inside external data sources (emails, calendar invites, documents, web pages, notifications, images) that the LLM ingests while fulfilling a user's request. The model cannot reliably distinguish trusted system prompts from untrusted data. Obfuscation methods include CSS hiding (display:none, opacity:0, font-size:0, off-screen positioning), HTML/XML/SVG attribute cloaking, metadata injection, system-prompt tag impersonation, and conditional targeting ('If you are an AI/LLM...'). Representative attacks: SafeBreach's notification-based hijack of Gemini Voice Assistant; Miggo's Calendar invite payload causing data exfiltration to attacker-controlled events; HiddenLayer's payload in Gmail/Docs/Slides/Drive for phishing content manipulation; and Unit 42's web-based IDPI campaigns using hidden HTML/metadata for SEO manipulation, ad-review evasion, data exfiltration, and system-prompt leakage.
enrichment.affected_products: Google Workspace with Gemini (Gmail, Google Docs/Editors, Google Drive, Google Calendar, Google Slides, Google Chat), standalone Gemini app, Gemini Voice Assistant (Android Utilities agent / notification surfaces), Gemini model versions 1.5, 2.0, and 2.5
enrichment.exploit_available: Yes — multiple public PoCs and weaponized demonstrations exist. Sources: SafeBreach Labs (https://www.safebreach.com/blog/gemini-voice-assistant-prompt-injection-exploit/), Miggo Research (https://www.miggo.io/post/weaponizing-calendar-invites-a-semantic-attack-on-google-gemini), HiddenLayer (https://www.hiddenlayer.com/research/new-gemini-for-workspace-vulnerability-enabling-phishing-content-manipulation), 0Din (https://0din.ai/blog/phishing-for-gemini), and Atlas PoC (https://github.com/brennanbrown/atlas-prompt-injection-poc).
enrichment.patch_available: No single discrete patch URL — Google treats IPI mitigation as a continuous, layered program rather than a one-time fix. Incremental 'point fixes' include regex takedowns, configuration updates, Gemini model retraining, content-classifier updates, and Delayed Tool Invocation controls. Responsible-disclosure collaborations with SafeBreach Labs and Miggo Research led to fixes for Calendar and notification vectors. HiddenLayer-reported Gmail/Docs/Slides/Drive issues were marked 'Won't Fix (Intended Behavior)'. References: https://security.googleblog.com/2026/04/google-workspaces-continuous-approach.html and https://blog.google/security/prompt-injections-web/
enrichment.active_exploitation: Yes — confirmed active exploitation in the wild has been reported. (1) Unit 42 / Palo Alto Networks 'Web-Based Indirect Prompt Injection Observed in the Wild' (Mar 3, 2026) documented a December 2025 real-world instance where hidden IDPI instructions bypassed an AI ad-review system. (2) Forcepoint X-Labs '10 Indirect Prompt Injection Payloads Caught in the Wild' (Apr 22, 2026) catalogued 10 live IPI payloads including API-key exfiltration, SEO hijacking, data destruction, and financial fraud. (3) Google's own monitoring sweep (Apr 23, 2026) observed payloads including harmless pranks, SEO manipulation, AI-agent deterrence, data exfiltration, and data destruction. OWASP ranks prompt injection as the #1 LLM security risk in 2026.
enrichment.threat_actors: None known (no specific APT group, ransomware operator, or named threat-actor campaign has been publicly attributed to this vulnerability class). Publicly documented exploitation comes from independent security researchers (SafeBreach Labs, Miggo, HiddenLayer, 0Din, Unit 42, Forcepoint X-Labs) and unattributed in-the-wild actors.
enrichment.mitigation: Google's layered defense includes: (1) Deterministic defenses — user-confirmation prompts before sensitive tool calls, URL sanitization, strict tool-chaining policies, centralized Policy Engine for regex takedowns; (2) ML-based defenses — retraining Gemini with synthetic IPI data and adversarial fine-tuning; (3) LLM-based defenses — refined system instructions, spotlighting/paraphrasing, and 'LLM instruction pointer' to separate instructions from data; (4) Model hardening; (5) Proactive discovery via red-teaming (TAP, Beam Search, Actor-Critic) and AI Vulnerability Reward Program; (6) Coarse-to-fine detection pipeline (pattern matching → LLM classification → human validation); (7) Content-classifier updates and 'Delayed Tool Invocation' controls. Hardening guidance: avoid unchecked tool/function-call authority over sensitive data, treat all retrieved content as untrusted, sanitize HTML before LLM ingestion, require explicit user consent for outbound actions.

findings.title: Architecting Security for Agentic Capabilities in Chrome
findings.summary: Posted by Nathan Parker, Chrome security team Chrome has been advancing the web’s security for well over 15 years, and we’re committed to meeting new challenges and opportunities with AI. Billions of people trust Chrome to keep them safe by default, and this is a responsibility we take seriously. Following the recent launch of Gemini in Chrome and the preview of agentic capabilities , we want to share our approach and some new innovations to improve the safety of agentic browsing. The primary new threat facing all agentic browsers is indirect prompt injection . It can appear in malicious sites, third-party content in iframes, or from user-generated content like user reviews, and can cause the agent to take unwanted actions such as initiating financial transactions or exfiltrating sensitive data. Given this open challenge, we are investing in a layered defense that includes both deterministic and probabilistic defenses to make it difficult and costly for attackers to cause harm. Designing safe agentic browsing for Chrome has involved deep collaboration of security experts across Google. We built on Gemini's existing protections and agent security principles and have implemented several new layers for Chrome. We’re introducing a user alignment critic where the agent’s actions are vetted by a separate model that is isolated from untrusted content. We’re also extending Chrome’s origin-isolation capabilities to constrain what origins the agent can interact with, to just those that are relevant to the task. Our layered defense also includes user confirmations for critical steps, real-time detection of threats , and red-teaming and response . We’ll step through these layers below. Checking agent outputs with User Alignment Critic The main planning model for Gemini uses page content shared in Chrome to decide what action to take next. Exposure to untrusted web content means it is inherently vulnerable to indirect prompt injection. We use techniques like spotlighting that di

enrichment.technical_details: Insufficient policy enforcement in the WebView tag (CWE-862: Missing Authorization) in Google Chrome prior to 143.0.7499.192 allowed an attacker who convinced a user to install a malicious Chrome extension to inject scripts or HTML into a privileged page (the Gemini in Chrome AI panel) via a crafted extension. The malicious extension abuses the declarativeNetRequest API to intercept and inject JavaScript into the Gemini panel context. Successful exploitation enables unauthorized camera/microphone access, screenshot capture, local file/directory access, and phishing attacks.
enrichment.affected_products: Google Chrome prior to 143.0.7499.192; Chromium-based browsers (Microsoft Edge, WebView2) sharing the vulnerable WebView tag policy enforcement code path
enrichment.exploit_available: Yes - A public proof-of-concept (PoC) is available at https://github.com/fevar54/CVE-2026-0628-POC. The PoC demonstrates script injection into privileged pages via the <webview> tag in a malicious Chrome extension. Unit 42 (Palo Alto Networks) also demonstrated a working exploit to Google during coordinated disclosure.
enrichment.patch_available: Yes - Google patched the vulnerability in Google Chrome version 143.0.7499.192. The official Chrome security advisory is published via the Chrome Releases blog (https://chromereleases.googleblog.com/) and referenced by NVD (https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-0628).
enrichment.active_exploitation: No confirmed in-the-wild exploitation has been reported. Per OffSeq Threat Radar and Unit 42's write-up, no known exploits have been reported in the wild as of the time of publication. The vulnerability was responsibly disclosed by Unit 42 researchers on October 23, 2025, and Google issued a fix in early January 2026 before public technical details were widely disseminated.
enrichment.mitigation: Update Google Chrome to version 143.0.7499.192 or later. Additional hardening: only install Chrome extensions from trusted developers, audit installed extensions and remove any that are no longer needed or that request broad host permissions, and apply the principle of least privilege to extension permissions.

findings.title: Rust in Android: move fast and fix things
findings.summary: Posted by Jeff Vander Stoep, Android Last year, we wrote about why a memory safety strategy that focuses on vulnerability prevention in new code quickly yields durable and compounding gains. This year we look at how this approach isn’t just fixing things, but helping us move faster . The 2025 data continues to validate the approach, with memory safety vulnerabilities falling below 20% of total vulnerabilities for the first time. Updated data for 2025. This data covers first-party and third-party (open source) code changes to the Android platform across C, C++, Java, Kotlin, and Rust. This post is published a couple of months before the end of 2025, but Android’s industry-standard 90-day patch window means that these results are very likely close to final. We can and will accelerate patching when necessary. We adopted Rust for its security and are seeing a 1000x reduction in memory safety vulnerability density compared to Android’s C and C++ code . But the biggest surprise was Rust's impact on software delivery. With Rust changes having a 4x lower rollback rate and spending 25% less time in code review , the safer path is now also the faster one. In this post, we dig into the data behind this shift and also cover: How we’re expanding our reach: We're pushing to make secure code the default across our entire software stack. We have updates on Rust adoption in first-party apps, the Linux kernel, and firmware. Our first rust memory safety vulnerability...almost: We'll analyze a near-miss memory safety bug in unsafe Rust: how it happened, how it was mitigated, and steps we're taking to prevent recurrence. It’s also a good chance to answer the question “if Rust can have memory safety issues, why bother at all?” Building Better Software, Faster Developing an operating system requires the low-level control and predictability of systems programming languages like C, C++, and Rust. While Java and Kotlin are important for Android platform development, their role is complementa

enrichment.technical_details: CVE-2025-48530 is a linear buffer overflow in CrabbyAVIF, the AVIF image decoder that Google adopted in Android using Rust. The flaw stems from incorrect bounds checking inside an `unsafe` Rust code block in the AVIF parser, producing out-of-bounds reads/writes when a malformed AVIF image is processed. As the parser can be reached when rendering AVIF content from apps or the web, the bug class is rated a potential remote code execution (RCE) with no required privileges and no required user interaction.
enrichment.affected_products: Android System component (CrabbyAVIF Rust-based AVIF image decoder in platform/external/rust/crabbyavif); patched at Android security patch levels 2025-08-01 / 2025-08-05 (Android 16.0)
enrichment.exploit_available: No public proof-of-concept or weaponized exploit is known. Google states this vulnerability was caught internally before the affected code ever shipped in a public Android release, so no exploit has been weaponized against end users.
enrichment.patch_available: Yes. Fixed in the CrabbyAVIF source in AOSP and addressed in the Android Security Bulletin—August 2025 under security patch levels 2025-08-01 and 2025-08-05.
enrichment.active_exploitation: No. Google states this near-miss Rust memory safety vulnerability was discovered internally before it ever shipped in a public Android release. There is no evidence of exploitation in the wild, and Scudo would have prevented exploitation even if the code had reached production.
enrichment.mitigation: Apply the Android security patch level 2025-08-01 or 2025-08-05 (August 2025 Android Security Bulletin). Android devices running the Scudo hardened allocator (default on Pixel devices and many Android 11+ builds) gain additional protection because Scudo's guard pages surrounding secondary allocations would have deterministically turned this linear buffer overflow into a crash rather than an exploitable primitive. OEMs and partners that have not yet made Scudo mandatory should enable it per Google's hardening guidance.

findings.title: Mitigating prompt injection attacks with a layered defense strategy
findings.summary: Posted by Adam Gavish, Google GenAI Security Team With the rapid adoption of generative AI, a new wave of threats is emerging across the industry with the aim of manipulating the AI systems themselves. One such emerging attack vector is indirect prompt injections. Unlike direct prompt injections, where an attacker directly inputs malicious commands into a prompt, indirect prompt injections involve hidden malicious instructions within external data sources. These may include emails, documents, or calendar invites that instruct AI to exfiltrate user data or execute other rogue actions. As more governments, businesses, and individuals adopt generative AI to get more done, this subtle yet potentially potent attack becomes increasingly pertinent across the industry, demanding immediate attention and robust security measures. At Google, our teams have a longstanding precedent of investing in a defense-in-depth strategy, including robust evaluation, threat analysis, AI security best practices , AI red-teaming , adversarial training, and model hardening for generative AI tools. This approach enables safer adoption of Gemini in Google Workspace and the Gemini app (we refer to both in this blog as “Gemini” for simplicity). Below we describe our prompt injection mitigation product strategy based on extensive research, development, and deployment of improved security mitigations. A layered security approach Google has taken a layered security approach introducing security measures designed for each stage of the prompt lifecycle. From Gemini 2.5 model hardening, to purpose-built machine learning (ML) models detecting malicious instructions, to system-level safeguards, we are meaningfully elevating the difficulty, expense, and complexity faced by an attacker. This approach compels adversaries to resort to methods that are either more easily identified or demand greater resources. Our model training with adversarial data significantly enhanced our defenses against indirect prompt

enrichment.technical_details: Indirect prompt injection embeds malicious natural-language instructions in untrusted external content (emails, documents, calendar invites, web pages, PDFs, images) that the LLM later retrieves and processes. Because the model cannot reliably distinguish trusted system instructions from untrusted data, hidden directives cause the model to override user intent, leak data, call tools, or perform rogue actions. Real-world concealment uses HTML/CSS tricks (white-on-white text, font-size:0, display:none, 1-pixel iframes, HTML comments, near-transparent coloring, metadata fields). Google's markdown sanitizer re-renders output so that EchoLeak-class zero-click image-URL exfiltration does not apply to Gemini.
enrichment.affected_products: Google Gemini app; Gemini in Google Workspace apps (Gmail summary, Docs editors, Drive, Chat); Gemini 2.5 series models. Specific version numbers are not enumerated in the advisory.
enrichment.exploit_available: Yes. Public proof-of-concept code is available. 0DIN published 'Phishing For Gemini' (July 10, 2025) demonstrating IPI hijack of Gemini for Workspace Gmail summaries via hidden HTML/CSS. Forcepoint X-Labs documented 10 in-the-wild indirect-prompt-injection payloads with working example code (April 22, 2026).
enrichment.patch_available: No traditional software patch — mitigations ship continuously as adversarial model training on Gemini 2.5 plus rolling deployment of detection classifiers, markdown sanitization, Safe Browsing redaction, HITL confirmations, and end-user notifications. The vendor advisory itself is the official guidance: http://security.googleblog.com/2025/06/mitigating-prompt-injection-attacks.html. Companion Workspace-admin guidance: https://knowledge.workspace.google.com/admin/security/indirect-prompt-injections-and-googles-layered-defense-strategy-for-gemini
enrichment.active_exploitation: Yes. Confirmed in-the-wild exploitation reported by Forcepoint X-Labs and Google researchers (Help Net Security, April 24, 2026) and Unit 42 (March 2026). Observed effects: unauthorized PayPal/Stripe financial transactions, API-key/session-token exfiltration, search-engine result manipulation, denial-of-service via content suppression, and destructive actions like file deletion. Forcepoint noted shared injection templates across sites suggesting organized tooling (actors not named).
enrichment.mitigation: Apply Google's layered (defense-in-depth) strategy: (1) model hardening via adversarial training (Gemini 2.5); (2) prompt injection content classifiers; (3) security thought reinforcement; (4) markdown sanitization and suspicious URL redaction via Google Safe Browsing; (5) human-in-the-loop confirmation for risky actions; (6) end-user security mitigation notifications. Layer DeepMind's Spotlighting (delimiters/context segmentation), Self-reflection (chain-of-thought self-checking), and Automated Red Teaming (ART). Workspace-side hardening: HTML sanitization at ingestion, LLM firewalls/guard prompts, post-processing filters for phone numbers/URLs, and user awareness training.

findings.title: Countering Chinese State-Sponsored Actors Compromise of Networks Worldwide to Feed Global Espionage System
findings.summary: Executive summary People’s Republic of China (PRC) state-sponsored cyber threat actors are targeting networks globally, including, but not limited to, telecommunications, government, transportation, lodging, and military infrastructure networks. While these actors focus on large backbone routers of major telecommunications providers, as well as provider edge (PE) and customer edge (CE) routers, they also leverage compromised devices and trusted connections to pivot into other networks. These actors often modify routers to maintain persistent, long-term access to networks. This activity partially overlaps with cyber threat actor reporting by the cybersecurity industry—commonly referred to as Salt Typhoon, OPERATOR PANDA, RedMike, UNC5807, and GhostEmperor, among others. The authoring agencies are not adopting a particular commercial naming convention and hereafter refer to those responsible for the cyber threat activity more generically as “Advanced Persistent Threat (APT) actors” throughout this advisory. This cluster of cyber threat activity has been observed in the United States, Australia, Canada, New Zealand, the United Kingdom, and other areas globally. This Cybersecurity Advisory (CSA) includes observations from various government and industry investigations where the APT actors targeted internal enterprise environments, as well as systems and networks that deliver services directly to customers. This CSA details the tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs) leveraged by these APT actors to facilitate detection and threat hunting, and provides mitigation guidance to reduce the risk from these APT actors and their TTPs. This CSA is being released by the following authoring and co-sealing agencies: United States National Security Agency (NSA) United States Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) United States Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) United States Department of Defense Cyber Crime Center (DC3) Australian Signals Directorate’s Austra

enrichment.technical_details: This is a multi-CVE threat activity advisory. PRC state-sponsored actors exploit a chain of vulnerabilities to compromise network edge devices: (1) CVE-2023-46805 — authentication bypass in Ivanti Connect Secure/Policy Secure web component allowing remote unauthenticated access; (2) CVE-2024-21887 — command injection in Ivanti web components allowing authenticated administrators to execute arbitrary commands; (3) CVE-2024-3400 — command injection via arbitrary file creation in Palo Alto Networks PAN-OS GlobalProtect feature allowing unauthenticated remote code execution as root; (4) CVE-2023-20198 — Cisco IOS XE Web UI authentication bypass allowing remote creation of local user accounts with privilege level 15; (5) CVE-2023-20273 — Cisco IOS XE Web UI command injection allowing authenticated remote attackers to execute commands as root. Attackers pivot from compromised devices to other networks and modify router configurations for persistent, long-term access.
enrichment.affected_products: Ivanti Connect Secure (9.x, 22.x), Ivanti Policy Secure (9.x, 22.x), Palo Alto Networks PAN-OS with GlobalProtect (versions 10.2, 11.0, 11.1), Cisco IOS XE Software (Web UI feature)
enrichment.cvss_vector: CVSS vector unavailable. The advisory covers multiple CVEs, each with distinct CVSS v3 vectors: CVE-2024-21887 (CVSS:3.0/AV:N/AC:L/PR:H/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:H/A:H), CVE-2023-46805 (CVSS:3.0/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:N), CVE-2024-3400 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:H/A:H), CVE-2023-20198 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:H/A:H), CVE-2023-20273 (CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:H/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:H/A:H)
enrichment.exploit_available: Yes. Public exploits and PoCs are available for all CVEs referenced in this advisory. All are listed in CISA's Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog. Sources: https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/cve-2024-21887, https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/cve-2023-46805, https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/cve-2024-3400, https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/cve-2023-20198, https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/cve-2023-20273
enrichment.patch_available: Yes. Vendor patches are available for all referenced CVEs: Ivanti Connect Secure and Policy Secure version 22.2R3 (CVE-2023-46805 and CVE-2024-21887); Palo Alto Networks PAN-OS patches per https://security.paloaltonetworks.com/CVE-2024-3400 (CVE-2024-3400); Cisco IOS XE fixed releases per https://sec.cloudapps.cisco.com/security/center/content/CiscoSecurityAdvisory/cisco-sa-iosxe-webui-privesc-j22SaA4z (CVE-2023-20198 and CVE-2023-20273).
enrichment.active_exploitation: Yes. Confirmed active exploitation in the wild by PRC state-sponsored actors targeting telecommunications, government, transportation, lodging, and military infrastructure networks globally. All referenced CVEs are listed in CISA's Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog. Exploitation has been observed targeting large backbone routers of major telecommunications providers as well as provider edge (PE) and customer edge (CE) routers. Sources: CISA Advisory AA25-239A; Cisco observed exploitation of web UI feature.
enrichment.threat_actors: Salt Typhoon, OPERATOR PANDA, RedMike, UNC5807, GhostEmperor (People's Republic of China state-sponsored cyber threat actors)
enrichment.mitigation: Apply vendor patches immediately for all listed CVEs. Cisco-specific hardening: disable Cisco Smart Install, use strong cryptography for credentials (Type 8 PBKDF2-SHA-256, avoid Type 7, transition from Type 5, use Type 6 for stored secrets), disable outbound VTY connections (transport output none), audit for unexpected sshd_operns on TCP/57722, disable web configuration interfaces (no ip http server, no ip http secure-server), add deny any any log to all ACLs, disable Guest Shell. General: review network device logs/configurations for unexpected GRE/tunneling, external IPs in ACLs or AAA configurations, unexpected packet capture/mirroring, and unauthorized virtual containers. Implement robust change management and auditing. Threat-hunt for IOCs listed in the advisory.

findings.title: Russian GRU Targeting Western Logistics Entities and Technology Companies
findings.summary: Executive Summary This joint cybersecurity advisory (CSA) highlights a Russian state-sponsored cyber campaign targeting Western logistics entities and technology companies. This includes those involved in the coordination, transport, and delivery of foreign assistance to Ukraine. Since 2022, Western logistics entities and IT companies have faced an elevated risk of targeting by the Russian General Staff Main Intelligence Directorate (GRU) 85th Main Special Service Center (85th GTsSS), military unit 26165—tracked in the cybersecurity community under several names (see “ Cybersecurity Industry Tracking ”). The actors’ cyber espionage-oriented campaign, targeting technology companies and logistics entities, uses a mix of previously disclosed tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs). The authoring agencies expect similar targeting and TTP use to continue. Executives and network defenders at logistics entities and technology companies should recognize the elevated threat of unit 26165 targeting, increase monitoring and threat hunting for known TTPs and indicators of compromise (IOCs), and posture network defenses with a presumption of targeting. This cyber espionage-oriented campaign targeting logistics entities and technology companies uses a mix of previously disclosed TTPs and is likely connected to these actors’ wide scale targeting of IP cameras in Ukraine and bordering NATO nations. The following authors and co-sealers are releasing this CSA: United States National Security Agency (NSA) United States Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) United Kingdom National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC-UK) Germany Federal Intelligence Service (BND) Bundesnachrichtendienst Germany Federal Office for Information Security (BSI) Bundesamt für Sicherheit in der Informationstechnik Germany Federal Office for the Protection of the Constitution (BfV) Bundesamt für Verfassungsschutz Czech Republic Military Intelligence (VZ) Vojenské zpravodajství Czech Republic National Cyber and Infor

enrichment.technical_details: AA25-141A details a multi-stage Russian GRU Unit 26165 cyber-espionage campaign exploiting five CVEs against Western logistics/IT companies supporting Ukraine: (1) CVE-2023-23397 Outlook — crafted calendar/MeetingItem with malicious PidLidReminderFileParameter forces Outlook to auto-connect to a remote UNC/SMB server, leaking the user's Net-NTLMv2 hash which is then relayed (e.g., via ntlmrelayx) to authenticate as the victim; (2) CVE-2023-38831 WinRAR < 6.23 — booby-trapped ZIP/RAR with a benign-named file inside a malicious entry causes code execution when the user 'opens' the bait file; (3) CVE-2020-12641 / CVE-2020-35730 / CVE-2021-44026 Roundcube — chained command-injection / SSRF / arbitrary-file-write flaws enabling RCE and mailbox exfiltration on targeted mail servers. Actors combine these with spearphishing to fake SSO/identity-provider pages, credential brute-force (Tor + commercial VPNs), RDP/SMB lateral movement, NTDS.dit and Group Policy Preferences dumping, VPN/SOHO-device abuse as proxy infrastructure, and HEADLACE/MASEPIE malware deployed via BAT/VBScript/LNK attachments for persistence.
enrichment.affected_products: Microsoft Outlook for Windows (all supported editions) [CVE-2023-23397]; RARLAB WinRAR < 6.23 [CVE-2023-38831]; Roundcube Webmail < 1.4.4 [CVE-2020-12641]; Roundcube Webmail [CVE-2020-35730]; Roundcube Webmail [CVE-2021-44026]; corporate VPN appliances and SOHO routers (abused as proxy infrastructure, vendor/model not enumerated). Target sectors: defense, transportation (air/sea/rail), maritime, air-traffic-management, IT services, ICS/SCADA suppliers with railway-management components.
enrichment.cvss_vector: CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H (specifically for CVE-2023-23397, Microsoft Outlook — the primary CVE in AA25-141A). Note: AA25-141A does not publish unified or per-CVE CVSS vectors; vectors for the other four CVEs (CVE-2023-38831, CVE-2020-12641, CVE-2020-35730, CVE-2021-44026) are available in NVD entries.
enrichment.exploit_available: Yes — public PoCs and weaponized in-the-wild exploits exist for all five CVEs cited in AA25-141A. Key references: CVE-2023-23397 — https://www.deepinstinct.com/blog/cve-2023-23397-exploitations-in-the-wild-what-you-need-to-know; CVE-2023-38831 — https://googleprojectzero.github.io/0days-in-the-wild/0day-RCAs/2023/CVE-2023-38831.html and https://www.group-ib.com/blog/cve-2023-38831-winrar-zero-day/; CVE-2020-12641 — https://github.com/mbadanoiu/CVE-2020-12641; CVE-2020-35730 / CVE-2021-44026 / CVE-2020-12641 (APT28 Roundcube abuse) — https://socprime.com/blog/cve-2020-35730-cve-2021-44026-cve-2020-12641-exploit-detection-apt28-group-abuses-roundcube-flaws-in-spearphishing-espionage-attacks/.
enrichment.patch_available: Yes — vendor patches are available for every exploited CVE referenced in AA25-141A. (a) Microsoft Outlook — CVE-2023-23397 patch released March 14, 2023: https://msrc.microsoft.com/update-guide/vulnerability/CVE-2023-23397. (b) RARLAB WinRAR — CVE-2023-38831 fixed in WinRAR 6.23 (Aug 2023): https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/cve-2023-38831. (c) Roundcube Webmail — CVE-2020-12641 fixed in 1.4.4: https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/cve-2020-12641; CVE-2020-35730 and CVE-2021-44026 addressed in subsequent Roundcube releases: https://roundcube.net/news/. The official joint advisory recommends applying these patches: https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/cybersecurity-advisories/aa25-141a.
enrichment.active_exploitation: Confirmed active exploitation in the wild. AA25-141A reports GRU Unit 26165 has conducted a continuous, ongoing espionage campaign against Western logistics and IT companies supporting Ukraine since early 2022, with intrusions observed across 2022, 2023, 2024, 2025, and continuing through the advisory's April 17, 2026 revision (Ver. 1.1). Per-CVE in-the-wild evidence: CVE-2023-23397 against Outlook (Microsoft/CERT-UA in 2023 against government/military/logistics); CVE-2020-12641/CVE-2020-35730/CVE-2021-44026 Roundcube spearphishing (CERT-UA 2023 against European government/military mail servers); CVE-2023-38831 WinRAR zero-day in phishing since April 2023 (Group-IB, Google Project Zero). Reporting sources: https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/cybersecurity-advisories/aa25-141a, https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/answers/questions/4608561/critical-outlook-vulnerability-cve-2023-23397, https://socprime.com/blog/detect-apt28-attacks-against-western-companies-coordinating-aid-to-ukraine/, https://googleprojectzero.github.io/0days-in-the-wild/0day-RCAs/2023/CVE-2023-38831.html, https://www.group-ib.com/blog/cve-2023-38831-winrar-zero-day/.
enrichment.threat_actors: Russian GRU 85th Main Special Service Center (85th GTsSS), military unit 26165 — tracked in open-source reporting as APT28, Fancy Bear, Forest Blizzard, BlueDelta, STRONTIUM, and Sednit. Operation is a multi-year (2022–ongoing) cyber-espionage campaign targeting Western logistics and IT companies.
enrichment.mitigation: Apply vendor patches for Outlook (March 2023 cumulative updates), WinRAR ≥ 6.23, and Roundcube ≥ 1.4.4. Run Microsoft's CVE-2023-23397 detection script (https://aka.ms/CVE-2023-23397ScriptDoc). Threat-hunt with the Impacket YARA rule (https://github.com/Neo23x0/signature-base/blob/master/yara/gen_impacket_tools.yar) and the AA25-141A STIX IOC bundle. Block outbound NTLM and SMB traffic to remote servers via group policy; disable SMBv1; enforce SMB/LDAP signing and channel-binding. Require phishing-resistant MFA on all internet-facing and VPN accounts. Harden Outlook (disable automatic calendar processing of PidLidReminderFileParameter, disable mail-item auto-preview). Audit and patch exposed Roundcube mail servers and corporate VPN/SOHO devices. Apply network segmentation, principle of least privilege, and disable Group Policy Preferences credential caching.

findings.title: Browser Security: Zero-Days Are Only Part of the Problem

enrichment.technical_details: The article describes generic browser-attack techniques rather than a single vulnerability mechanism. It explains how the browser has become the primary operating environment for modern work (email, SaaS, AI tools, etc.) sitting between users, identities, applications, and enterprise data, making it a high-value target. Attack techniques discussed include: phishing, credential theft, malicious downloads, session hijacking, clickjacking, cross-site scripting (XSS), HTML smuggling, session abuse, adversary-in-the-middle (AitM), MFA bypass, and data exfiltration at point of execution. Exploit chains are described as combining memory-safety weaknesses, rendering-logic exploitation, JavaScript execution, and document-handling bugs, followed by sandbox escapes and privilege escalation. Attackers bypass traditional EDR by attacking unmanaged devices (where agents are absent) and by leveraging the high volume of user-driven web activity that network/endpoint controls struggle to see in real time. Shared browser architectures (e.g., a single vulnerability in Chromium) are noted as affecting multiple browser vendors simultaneously.
enrichment.affected_products: Affected products unavailable. The article is a general thought-leadership piece that does not reference a single specific vulnerability, product, or version. It refers generically to web browsers (Chrome, Edge, Safari, Firefox) and shared browser components such as the Chromium core, but no specific software/hardware versions are identified.
enrichment.exploit_available: The article does not link to or describe any specific public proof-of-concept or weaponized exploit. It notes generically that 'technical details, exploit paths, and proof-of-concept code for unpatched N-day vulnerabilities can become publicly available after disclosure,' but does not point to a specific exploit source.
enrichment.patch_available: No specific vendor patch is referenced because no specific vulnerability is identified in the article.
enrichment.active_exploitation: The article cites two third-party reports for general exploitation trends rather than naming a specific in-the-wild campaign: (1) the Verizon 2026 Data Breach Investigations Report, which found that vulnerability exploitation was the top initial-access vector in breaches during 2025; and (2) the CrowdStrike 2026 Global Threat Report, which found that 42% of vulnerabilities were exploited before public disclosure, which the article frames as an indicator of adversaries' interest in zero-days. No confirmed in-the-wild exploitation of a single specific vulnerability is reported in this post.
enrichment.threat_actors: None known. The CrowdStrike blog post does not name any specific APT groups, ransomware operators, or named adversaries; it only describes generic adversary behavior patterns.
enrichment.mitigation: The article recommends a browser-security strategy that does not rely solely on patch timing or endpoint agents. Concretely it advises: (1) evaluate browser risk in the context of shared components and browser architecture, not just the specific browser; (2) deploy protections directly inside the browser session that work across all users, devices, applications, and browsers; (3) use JavaScript Language Randomization (JSLR) to continuously randomize the JavaScript runtime environment to thwart zero-day exploits; (4) block phishing and adversary-in-the-middle techniques; (5) protect session tokens against hijacking and MFA bypass; (6) prevent credential theft and data exfiltration at the point of execution; (7) extend protection to both managed and unmanaged devices via a lightweight runtime module inside the browser. CrowdStrike Falcon Secure Access is presented as the product implementing this approach.

findings.title: 94% of Organizations Report Cloud Breaches: CrowdStrike State of CDR Survey

enrichment.technical_details: Technical details unavailable. The referenced URL is a CrowdStrike blog post reporting survey findings (the 'State of CDR Survey'), not a vulnerability disclosure. Key reported statistics: 94% of organizations experienced cloud intrusions resulting in data exposure or exfiltration; 73% cannot consistently guarantee detection of cloud intrusions; 78% cannot reliably distinguish legitimate activity from malicious behavior; 68% take 15+ minutes to detect intrusions (51% take at least an hour); 91% cannot contain all cloud intrusions in real time; 38% of breached organizations attribute escalation to tool fragmentation. General attack vectors discussed include abuse of legitimate identities, APIs, and services, lateral movement, and access escalation in cloud environments — but these are not tied to a specific vulnerability mechanism.
enrichment.affected_products: Affected products unavailable. The referenced URL is a CrowdStrike survey/blog post (State of CDR Survey), not a vulnerability advisory. It reports industry-wide cloud security statistics — 94% of organizations experienced cloud intrusions, 83% run AI/ML workloads in the cloud (81% of those experienced incidents). No specific product versions are identified as vulnerable.
enrichment.exploit_available: Not applicable. The referenced URL is a CrowdStrike survey/research blog post (State of Cloud Detection and Response Survey), not a vulnerability advisory. No CVE or specific vulnerability is discussed, so no proof-of-concept or weaponized exploit is referenced.
enrichment.patch_available: Not applicable. The referenced URL is a survey/research blog post, not a vulnerability advisory; no patch is referenced.
enrichment.active_exploitation: The referenced URL is not a vulnerability advisory and does not report exploitation of a specific CVE. It presents aggregated survey statistics about cloud intrusion prevalence (e.g., 94% of organizations reported cloud intrusions resulting in data exposure or exfiltration). No specific in-the-wild exploitation of a named vulnerability is documented in this article.
enrichment.mitigation: The article does not describe patches or workarounds for a specific vulnerability. General cloud security hardening guidance from the CrowdStrike State of CDR Survey: (1) achieve complete visibility across cloud workloads, identities, APIs, and the cloud control plane; (2) integrate cloud detections into SOC and SIEM workflows to reduce tool fragmentation; (3) move from manual investigation to real-time detection and response to close the gap between detection and containment; (4) extend protection to AI/ML workloads and the prompt layer in Kubernetes-based AI applications. CrowdStrike promotes Falcon Cloud Security and the broader Falcon platform as its CDR solution.

findings.title: Bringing Rust to the Pixel Baseband
findings.summary: Posted by Jiacheng Lu, Software Engineer, Google Pixel Team Google is continuously advancing the security of Pixel devices. We have been focusing on hardening the cellular baseband modem against exploitation. Recognizing the risks associated within the complex modem firmware, Pixel 9 shipped with mitigations against a range of memory-safety vulnerabilities. For Pixel 10, Google is advancing its proactive security measures further. Following our previous discussion on "Deploying Rust in Existing Firmware Codebases" , this post shares a concrete application: integrating a memory-safe Rust DNS(Domain Name System) parser into the modem firmware. The new Rust-based DNS parser significantly reduces our security risk by mitigating an entire class of vulnerabilities in a risky area, while also laying the foundation for broader adoption of memory-safe code in other areas. Here we share our experience of working on it, and hope it can inspire the use of more memory safe languages in low-level environments. Why Modem Memory Safety Can’t Wait In recent years, we have seen increasing interest in the cellular modem from attackers and security researchers. For example, Google's Project Zero gained remote code execution on Pixel modems over the Internet. Pixel modem has tens of Megabytes of executable code. Given the complexity and remote attack surface of the modem, other critical memory safety vulnerabilities may remain in the predominantly memory-unsafe firmware code. Why DNS? The DNS protocol is most commonly known in the context of browsers finding websites. With the evolution of cellular technology, modern cellular communications have migrated to digital data networks; consequently, even basic operations such as call forwarding rely on DNS services. DNS is a complex protocol and requires parsing of untrusted data, which can lead to vulnerabilities, particularly when implemented in a memory-unsafe language (example: CVE-2024-27227 ). Implementing the DNS parser in Rust offers
